title: AI增强化学成像与蛋白质相互作用动态监测验证
domain: AI-enhanced chemical imaging methods for dynamic monitoring of protein-protein interactions
objective: 验证 ZhiZhi → TanXi → MingLi 从文献检索、知识图谱、gap发现到可验证假设生成的闭环。

papergraph.title: Live-cell imaging analysis of the intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligand using newly developed fluorescence-labeled molecular probes based on trehalose diester
papergraph.citation: Takanori Matsumaru et al. (2025) Live-cell imaging analysis of the intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligand using newly developed fluorescence-labeled molecular probes based on trehalose diester doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-w7lbt
papergraph.abstract: Mincle, a member of the C-type lectin receptor (CLR) family, detects various glycolipids and glycerolipids, such as trehalose dimycolate (TDM) from Mycobacterium tuberculosis, leading to the activation of the innate immune system. In this study, we developed new fluorescence-labeled molecular probes, N-NBD-labeled TDE and O-NBD-labeled TDE, based on the structure of trehalose diester to elucidate the intracellular behavior of Mincle and its ligands. N-NBD-labeled TDE was prepared for the ligand analysis, and O-NBD-labeled TDE was specifically designed to label Mincle by Turn-ON fluorescent affinity labeling. Live-cell imaging analysis using these probes revealed that N-NBD-labeled TDE internalizes into the cell in a Mincle-dependent manner. Furthermore, imaging analysis using O-NBD-labeled TDE successfully detected Mincle in cells in a Mincle expression-dependent manner.
papergraph.scenario: intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligands
papergraph.contribution: Development of Turn-ON fluorescent affinity labeling probe (O-NBD-labeled TDE) enabling specific detection of Mincle in live cells, and demonstration of Mincle-dependent internalization of ligand probe (N-NBD-labeled TDE)
papergraph.limitation: Full text excerpt: Development of Turn-ON fluorescent affinity labeling probe (O-NBD-labeled TDE) enabling specific detection of Mincle in live cells, and demonstration of Mincle-dependent internalization of ligand probe (N-NBD-labeled TDE) No explicit limitation extracted.

papergraph.title: Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis
papergraph.citation: Pia Lopez et al. (2024) Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-kl8mp
papergraph.abstract: Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) is an emerging tool to characterize ongoing chemical reactions in synthetic chemistry and catalysis. Initially applied to biological systems, FLIM now reveals spatially resolved chemical reaction species and system-wide physiochemical changes that accompany ongoing reactions. FLIM combines the advantage of environmental sensitivity with high signal sensitivity (as sensitive as single molecules) and has the key ability to operate under synthetic conditions (e.g., high concentrations of reagents, in organic solvents, under ambient temperature and pressure, in opaque mixtures, and in multiphasic systems). Chemical reactions inherently induce changes in the reaction medium, neighboring compounds, surface compositions, and/or the bonding structure of the compounds involved, resulting in environmental changes. The FLIM methods recently developed harness and interpret these changes in ways that lead to characterizing compounds and enhancing mechanistic understanding. Here, current advantages and limitations of FLIM method are considered, common factors influencing fluorescence lifetime in chemical systems are discussed in a tutorial format, and seven research case studies are strategically analyzed—chosen to highlight how FLIM provided complementary information to understand chemical reaction mechanisms, intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, roles of reagents, and catalyst behaviors. These data and insights obtained from FLIM assist the rational design and optimization of synthetic and catalytic methods.
strengths: high signal sensitivity (as sensitive as single molecules)
ability to operate under synthetic conditions (e.g., high concentrations of reagents, in organic solvents, under ambient temperature and pressure, in opaque mixtures, and in multiphasic systems)
improvements: current advantages and limitations of FLIM method are considered
common factors influencing fluorescence lifetime in chemical systems are discussed in a tutorial format
seven research case studies are strategically analyzed
papergraph.contribution: FLIM provides complementary information to understand chemical reaction mechanisms, intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, roles of reagents, and catalyst behaviors, assisting rational design and optimization of synthetic and catalytic methods
papergraph.limitation: current advantages and limitations of FLIM method are considered

gap_signals.text: Full text excerpt: FLIM provides complementary information to understand chemical reaction mechanisms, intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, roles of reagents, and catalyst behaviors, assisting rational design and optimization of synthetic and catalytic methods current advantages and limitations of FLIM method are considered

papergraph.title: Locally-Activated Chemogenetic pH Probes for Monitoring Protein Exocytosis
papergraph.citation: Justine Coïs et al. (2024) Locally-Activated Chemogenetic pH Probes for Monitoring Protein Exocytosis doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-rcfzm
papergraph.abstract: Genetically encoded biosensors based on fluorescent proteins are valuable tools for imaging biological processes with high selectivity. In particular, pH-sensitive fluorescent proteins such as the GFP-derived superecliptic pHluorin (SEP) or pHuji allow tracking of protein trafficking through acidic and neutral compartments. Recently, chemogenetic indicators combining synthetic fluorophores with genetically encoded self-labeling protein tags (SLP-tag) offer a versatile alternative that combines the diversity of chemical probes and indicators with the selectivity of the genetic-encoding. Here, we describe a novel fluorogenic and chemogenetic pH sensor consisting of a cell-permeable molecular pH indicator called pHluo-Halo-1 whose fluorescence can be locally activated in cells by reaction with the SLP-tag HaloTag ensuring high signal selectivity in wash-free imaging experiments. pHluo-Halo display a good pH sensitivity and a suitable pKa of 5.9 to monitor biological pH variations. This hybrid chemogenetic pH probe was applied to follow the exocytosis of a CD63-HaloTag fusion proteins enabling the visualization of exosomes released from acidic vesicles into the extracellular media using TIRF microscopy. This chemogenetic platform is expected to be a powerful and versatile tool for elucidating the dynamics and regulatory mechanisms of proteins in living cells.
strengths: high signal selectivity in wash-free imaging experiments
suitable pKa of 5.9 to monitor biological pH variations
papergraph.method: chemogenetic pH sensing with pHluo-Halo-1 and HaloTag
papergraph.contribution: novel fluorogenic and chemogenetic pH sensor enabling wash-free, locally activated imaging of exocytosis
papergraph.limitation: Full text excerpt: novel fluorogenic and chemogenetic pH sensor enabling wash-free, locally activated imaging of exocytosis No explicit limitation extracted.

In particular, pH-sensitive fluorescent proteins such as the GFP-derived superecliptic pHluorin (SEP) or pHuji allow tracking of protein trafficking through acidic and neutral compartments. Recently, chemogenetic indicators combining synthetic fluorophores with genetically encoded self-labeling protein tags (SLP-tag) offer a versatile alternative that combines the diversity of chemical probes and indicators with the selectivity of the genetic-encoding. papergraph.title: Visual monitoring of biomolecular self-assembly dynamics and imaging of protein aggregates by distinct emission of a unique hydrophobic carbon dot
papergraph.citation: Debanjan Bagchi et al. (2025) Visual monitoring of biomolecular self-assembly dynamics and imaging of protein aggregates by distinct emission of a unique hydrophobic carbon dot doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-v5z81
papergraph.abstract: Here, we report a novel and unique application of a special hydrophobic carbon dot (HCD) to monitor biomolecular self-assembly along with the detection of metastable intermediates and self-aggregates. We exploited the restricted rotation of the S-S bond of the HCD synthesized from dithiosalicylic acid and melamine to exhibit different emission behaviors during the self-assembly of amino acids and proteins. The HCD that exhibits blue emission in amino acid droplets or protein aggregates dynamically changes its emission from blue to red with the time course of the amino acid self-assembly process. This unique and distinct change in emission can be visualized by the naked eye under a UV lamp. The ability of HCD to distinguish the biomolecular self-aggregated structures and monitor the self-assembly dynamics can be utilized for the visual detection of uncontrolled aggregation of proteins and peptides related to neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s disease.
strengths: Abstract: Here, we report a novel and unique application of a special hydrophobic carbon dot (HCD) to monitor biomolecular self-assembly along with the detection of metastable intermediates and self-aggregates. We exploited the restricted rotation of the S-S bond of the HCD synthesized from dithiosa...[truncated]
papergraph.contribution: A hydrophobic carbon dot enables naked-eye visual monitoring of biomolecular self-assembly dynamics and imaging of protein aggregates via distinct, time-dependent emission changes.

papergraph.title: A Multiscale Computational and Experimental Study of TBA–Fluorescent Probes for Protein Sensing: Photobasicity Over Twisted Intramolecular Charge Transfer as a New Mechanism for Protein Induced Fluorescence Enhancement
papergraph.citation: Makay T. Murray et al. (2025) A Multiscale Computational and Experimental Study of TBA–Fluorescent Probes for Protein Sensing: Photobasicity Over Twisted Intramolecular Charge Transfer as a New Mechanism for Protein Induced Fluorescence Enhancement doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-zfcb5
papergraph.abstract: Fluorescent probes are powerful tools for the detection of proteins in biomedical applications. However, the design of selectively active fluorescent probes is challenging due in part to difficulties predicting the functions of novel modifications, especially in different cellular environments. In the present study, a family of cyclic N-glycol-linked 4-formyl-aniline probes (denoted AnMeInd, AnMeBtz, and AnBtz), which have distinct thrombin binding affinities and fluorescent responses, were investigated at the T3 position of the thrombin binding aptamer (TBA) using TD-DFT calculations and classical and driven-adaptive bias molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. Classical MD and D-ABMD simulations corroborate the experimentally observed differences in thrombin binding affinities relative to canonical TBA, highlighting that neutral, shorter probes that lack an abundance of exocyclic substituents are better accommodated in a thrombin binding pocket that is rich in aromatic and charged residues. TD-DFT suggests that cationic AnMeInd and AnMeBtz exhibit the same inherent tendency to undergo twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT). However, the methyl substituents of AnMeInd reduce probe contacts with the aptamer scaffold to enhance TICT in the unbound state, and adjust probe binding location in the thrombin hydrophobic pocket, which increases solvent shielding, probe rigidity, and TICT suppression upon target binding compared to AnMeBtz. Unlike the cationic analogues, combined computational and experimental data suggest the best AnBtz probe functions as a photobase. Although AnBtz undergoes protonation in the excited state in solvent exposed environments (i.e., unbound TBA), which facilitates nonradiative decay, this pathway is suppressed upon thrombin binding due to reduced solvent accessibility, thereby enhancing fluorescence. These findings underscore the importance of tuning probe charge, linker length, exocyclic substituents, flexibility, and microenvironment in both the unbound and bound states to optimize fluorescence response. Our results provide a strategic foundation for designing high-performance fluorescent probes for biosensing and nucleic acid-based diagnostics.
strengths: Abstract: Fluorescent probes are powerful tools for the detection of proteins in biomedical applications. However, the design of selectively active fluorescent probes is challenging due in part to difficulties predicting the functions of novel modifications, especially in different cellular environm...[truncated]
papergraph.scenario: thrombin protein sensing | fluorescent probe design | TBA-aptamer conjugates
papergraph.contribution: Photobasicity—not twisted intramolecular charge transfer—is identified as the dominant mechanism for protein-induced fluorescence enhancement in the AnBtz probe, establishing a new design principle for fluorescent aptamer probes.
papergraph.limitation: However, the design of selectively active fluorescent probes is challenging due in part to difficulties predicting the functions of novel modifications, especially in different cellular environments.

keynotes.title: Live-cell imaging analysis of the intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligand using newly developed fluorescence-labeled molecular probes based on trehalose diester

keynote.title: Live-cell imaging analysis of the intracellular behavior of C-type lectin receptor Mincle and its ligand using newly developed fluorescence-labeled molecular probes based on trehalose diester
keynote.core_problem: Elucidating the intracellular behavior of Mincle and its ligands in live cells, particularly ligand internalization and receptor localization, due to lack of tools for real-time, specific detection.
contributions: Development of two novel fluorescence-labeled trehalose diester (TDE) probes: N-NBD-labeled TDE for ligand tracking and O-NBD-labeled TDE as a Turn-ON fluorescent affinity labeling probe for Mincle detection.
Demonstration that N-NBD-labeled TDE internalizes in a Mincle-dependent manner.
Demonstration that O-NBD-labeled TDE detects Mincle in a Mincle expression-dependent manner in live cells.
methods: Chemical synthesis of N-NBD-labeled TDE and O-NBD-labeled TDE probes.
Live-cell fluorescence imaging.
Mincle-dependence validation via comparison across cells with varying Mincle expression levels.
experiments_or_evidence: Live-cell imaging showing internalization of N-NBD-labeled TDE is abolished or reduced in Mincle-deficient or low-expressing cells.
Live-cell imaging showing fluorescence signal from O-NBD-labeled TDE correlates with Mincle expression level.
assumptions: Mincle expression level directly correlates with functional receptor availability for probe binding.
Turn-ON fluorescence activation of O-NBD-labeled TDE occurs selectively upon binding to Mincle, not nonspecifically.

gap_signals.text: Limited understanding of intracellular trafficking dynamics of Mincle–ligand complexes in native, unperturbed cellular environments.
Prior absence of live-cell-compatible, specific fluorescent probes for real-time tracking of Mincle and its ligands.

important_claims.claim: N-NBD-labeled TDE internalizes in a Mincle-dependent manner.
important_claims.evidence: Live-cell imaging analysis revealed internalization only in Mincle-expressing cells; absence or reduction in Mincle-deficient/low-expressing cells.

important_claims.claim: O-NBD-labeled TDE enables specific detection of Mincle in live cells via Turn-ON fluorescent affinity labeling.
important_claims.evidence: Fluorescence signal from O-NBD-labeled TDE was observed only in cells expressing Mincle and scaled with expression level.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides chemically defined, validated fluorescent probes for live-cell interrogation of CLR–ligand interactions; methodology adaptable to other lectin–glycolipid systems.

keynotes.title: Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis

keynote.title: Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) as a Tool to Understand Chemical Reactions and Catalysis
keynote.core_problem: Characterizing spatially resolved chemical reaction species and system-wide physiochemical changes during ongoing synthetic chemical reactions and catalytic processes.
contributions: Demonstrates FLIM as a viable, environment-sensitive imaging tool for synthetic chemistry and catalysis — beyond its traditional biological applications.
Provides a tutorial on factors influencing fluorescence lifetime in chemical systems.
Analyzes seven case studies showing how FLIM delivers complementary mechanistic insights (e.g., intermediates, product distributions, partitioning, reagent roles, catalyst behavior).
Highlights FLIM’s operational compatibility with synthetic conditions (high reagent concentrations, organic solvents, ambient T/P, opaque/multiphasic systems).
methods: Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM)
Interpretation of fluorescence lifetime changes induced by reaction-driven environmental perturbations (e.g., polarity, viscosity, pH, binding, quenching, molecular conformation)
experiments_or_evidence: Seven strategically selected research case studies illustrating FLIM application in synthetic chemistry and catalysis.
Evidence of FLIM detecting spatially resolved reaction species and physiochemical changes under realistic synthetic conditions.
assumptions: Fluorescence lifetime is sensitive and quantitatively interpretable with respect to local chemical environment changes during reactions.
Fluorophores (endogenous or introduced) remain photophysically stable and report reliably under synthetic conditions.

gap_signals.text: current advantages and limitations of FLIM method are considered

important_claims.claim: FLIM operates effectively under synthetic conditions including high reagent concentrations, organic solvents, ambient temperature and pressure, opaque mixtures, and multiphasic systems.
important_claims.evidence: Stated directly in abstract as a key ability of FLIM methods recently developed.

important_claims.claim: FLIM provides spatially resolved characterization of reaction species and system-wide physiochemical changes accompanying ongoing reactions.
important_claims.evidence: Stated directly in abstract as FLIM's emerging role in synthetic chemistry and catalysis.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — serves as a cross-disciplinary methodological bridge between microscopy, physical chemistry, and reaction engineering; enables spatially resolved, label-compatible (or label-free) monitoring of reactions without requiring transparent or dilute systems.

keynotes.title: Locally-Activated Chemogenetic pH Probes for Monitoring Protein Exocytosis

keynote.title: Locally-Activated Chemogenetic pH Probes for Monitoring Protein Exocytosis
keynote.core_problem: Monitoring protein exocytosis—particularly exosome release from acidic vesicles—with high spatiotemporal selectivity and without wash steps.
contributions: A novel fluorogenic chemogenetic pH sensor (pHluo-Halo-1) enabling local, covalent activation via HaloTag.
Wash-free imaging capability for tracking exocytosis in live cells.
Application to visualize CD63-HaloTag exosome release using TIRF microscopy.
A pKa-tuned probe (pKa = 5.9) suitable for detecting pH shifts across acidic-to-neutral transitions during exocytosis.
methods: Chemogenetic design: cell-permeable synthetic pH indicator (pHluo-Halo-1) conjugated to HaloTag via covalent labeling.
Fluorogenic activation: fluorescence only upon reaction with HaloTag, enabling spatial restriction.
Live-cell TIRF microscopy for imaging exocytosis of CD63-HaloTag fusion proteins.
pH calibration and sensitivity characterization (pKa = 5.9).
experiments_or_evidence: TIRF imaging of CD63-HaloTag exocytosis showing extracellular appearance of fluorescent signal upon vesicle fusion.
Demonstration of wash-free imaging enabled by local activation.
Reported pH sensitivity and pKa of 5.9 (implied via calibration, though no protocol or validation data described in excerpt).
assumptions: HaloTag expression and labeling are specific and non-disruptive to CD63 trafficking and exosome biogenesis.
pHluo-Halo-1 fluorescence response accurately reflects local pH changes during exocytosis (e.g., vesicle lumen acidification → neutralization upon fusion).
Background fluorescence from unreacted pHluo-Halo-1 is negligible in wash-free conditions.

gap_signals.text: Existing chemogenetic probes lack fluorogenicity and pH sensitivity tuned for acidic organelle-to-extracellular transitions.
Prior pH sensors (e.g., SEP, pHuji) require genetic encoding of fluorescent proteins and suffer from constitutive fluorescence, limiting signal-to-background in wash-free exocytosis assays.

important_claims.claim: pHluo-Halo-1 enables wash-free, locally activated imaging of exocytosis.
important_claims.evidence: Full text excerpt states: 'novel fluorogenic and chemogenetic pH sensor enabling wash-free, locally activated imaging of exocytosis'.

important_claims.claim: pHluo-Halo-1 has a pKa of 5.9, suitable for monitoring biological pH variations across acidic compartments.
important_claims.evidence: Explicitly stated: 'pHluo-Halo display a good pH sensitivity and a suitable pKa of 5.9 to monitor biological pH variations.'

important_claims.claim: The probe was applied to visualize exosome release from acidic vesicles using CD63-HaloTag and TIRF microscopy.
important_claims.evidence: Explicitly stated: 'applied to follow the exocytosis of a CD63-HaloTag fusion proteins enabling the visualization of exosomes released from acidic vesicles into the extracellular media using TIRF microscopy.'

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — provides a modular, fluorogenic chemogenetic scaffold (HaloTag + synthetic pH dye) that can be adapted to other membrane-trafficking proteins and pH-sensitive processes beyond exocytosis, especially where wash-free, background-suppressed imaging is critical.

keynotes.title: Locally-Activated Chemogenetic pH Probes for Monitoring Protein Exocytosis

keynote.title: Locally-Activated Chemogenetic pH Probes for Monitoring Protein Exocytosis
keynote.core_problem: Monitoring protein exocytosis with high spatiotemporal selectivity in living cells, particularly from acidic compartments into extracellular space, without wash steps that disrupt dynamics.
contributions: A novel fluorogenic chemogenetic pH sensor (pHluo-Halo-1) enabling local, covalent activation via HaloTag.
Wash-free imaging capability for real-time tracking of exocytosis.
Demonstration of exosome release visualization using CD63-HaloTag and TIRF microscopy.
methods: Design and synthesis of pHluo-Halo-1 — a cell-permeable, fluorogenic molecular pH indicator reactive with HaloTag.
Genetic fusion of HaloTag to CD63 for targeted labeling.
Total internal reflection fluorescence (TIRF) microscopy for surface-proximal exocytosis imaging.
experiments_or_evidence: Fluorescence activation of pHluo-Halo-1 specifically upon HaloTag conjugation in cells.
pH-dependent fluorescence response with measured pKa of 5.9.
Imaging of CD63-HaloTag exocytosis: visualization of exosome release from acidic vesicles into extracellular media using TIRF.
assumptions: HaloTag expression and localization accurately report CD63 trafficking.
pHluo-Halo-1’s pKa (5.9) appropriately reflects pH shifts during vesicle fusion and exposure to extracellular pH (~7.4).
Fluorogenic activation is specific to HaloTag and not subject to off-target labeling or background hydrolysis.

gap_signals.text: Prior genetically encoded pH sensors (e.g., SEP, pHuji) lack fluorogenicity and require wash steps or suffer from cytosolic background; chemogenetic alternatives were not locally activatable in situ.
Direct imaging of exosome release from acidic compartments into extracellular space was previously challenging due to poor signal-to-noise and inability to distinguish fused vs. unfused vesicles.

important_claims.claim: pHluo-Halo-1 enables wash-free, locally activated imaging of exocytosis.
important_claims.evidence: Full text excerpt states: 'novel fluorogenic and chemogenetic pH sensor enabling wash-free, locally activated imaging of exocytosis'.

important_claims.claim: pHluo-Halo-1 has a pKa of 5.9 suitable for monitoring biological pH variations.
important_claims.evidence: Abstract states: 'pHluo-Halo display a good pH sensitivity and a suitable pKa of 5.9 to monitor biological pH variations.'

important_claims.claim: The probe was applied to visualize exosome release from acidic vesicles into extracellular media.
important_claims.evidence: Abstract states: 'applied to follow the exocytosis of a CD63-HaloTag fusion proteins enabling the visualization of exosomes released from acidic vesicles into the extracellular media using TIRF microscopy.'

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High — provides a modular, chemogenetic platform adaptable to other HaloTag-fused proteins and potentially extendable to other self-labeling tags (e.g., SNAP-tag) with compatible fluorogenic ligands.

keynotes.title: Visual monitoring of biomolecular self-assembly dynamics and imaging of protein aggregates by distinct emission of a unique hydrophobic carbon dot

keynote.title: Visual monitoring of biomolecular self-assembly dynamics and imaging of protein aggregates by distinct emission of a unique hydrophobic carbon dot
keynote.core_problem: Real-time, label-free visual monitoring of biomolecular self-assembly dynamics and detection of metastable intermediates and protein aggregates—particularly relevant to neurodegenerative disease pathology.
contributions: Introduction of a hydrophobic carbon dot (HCD) with S-S bond-restricted rotation for ratiometric emission response to self-assembly state
Demonstration of naked-eye-detectable blue-to-red emission shift during amino acid/protein self-assembly under UV light
Application of HCD for distinguishing aggregated structures and tracking temporal dynamics of self-assembly
methods: Synthesis of hydrophobic carbon dot (HCD) from dithiosalicylic acid and melamine
Exploitation of restricted rotation around the S-S bond to modulate fluorescence emission
Fluorescence spectral monitoring under UV excitation during self-assembly of amino acids and proteins
Visual assessment of emission color change (blue → red) in droplets and aggregates
experiments_or_evidence: Observation of time-dependent blue-to-red emission shift in amino acid droplets
Imaging of protein aggregates with distinct HCD emission signatures
Naked-eye visualization under UV lamp confirming dynamic emission change
assumptions: Emission shift is directly correlated with conformational or microenvironmental changes during self-assembly
HCD selectively interacts with or reports on hydrophobic, aggregation-prone regions without perturbing native assembly pathways

gap_signals.text: Lack of quantitative kinetic modeling or calibration against established aggregation assays (e.g., ThT, CD, TEM)
No evidence provided on performance across diverse protein types, pH, ionic strength, or complex biological matrices

important_claims.claim: HCD exhibits blue emission in amino acid droplets or protein aggregates and shifts to red over time during self-assembly
important_claims.evidence: Reported observation of time-dependent blue-to-red emission shift visualized under UV lamp

important_claims.claim: The emission change enables visual detection of uncontrolled protein/peptide aggregation linked to Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s diseases
important_claims.evidence: Stated utility for visual detection; no experimental validation in disease-relevant models or patient-derived samples reported

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High potential as a low-cost, instrument-free optical probe for qualitative real-time aggregation screening in vitro; limited without characterization of selectivity, photostability, batch reproducibility, or compatibility with live systems.

keynotes.title: A Multiscale Computational and Experimental Study of TBA–Fluorescent Probes for Protein Sensing: Photobasicity Over Twisted Intramolecular Charge Transfer as a New Mechanism for Protein Induced Fluorescence Enhancement

keynote.title: A Multiscale Computational and Experimental Study of TBA–Fluorescent Probes for Protein Sensing: Photobasicity Over Twisted Intramolecular Charge Transfer as a New Mechanism for Protein Induced Fluorescence Enhancement
keynote.core_problem: Designing selectively active fluorescent probes for protein sensing is challenging due to difficulties predicting how novel probe modifications function—especially in varying cellular environments.
contributions: Identifies photobasicity (excited-state protonation) — not TICT — as the dominant fluorescence enhancement mechanism for the AnBtz probe upon thrombin binding.
Demonstrates that probe charge, linker length, exocyclic substituents, flexibility, and microenvironment in both unbound and bound states critically tune fluorescence response.
Provides a multiscale framework integrating TD-DFT, classical MD, and driven-adaptive bias MD (D-ABMD) to rationalize structure–function relationships in aptamer–probe–protein systems.
methods: Time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations
Classical molecular dynamics (MD) simulations
Driven-adaptive bias molecular dynamics (D-ABMD) simulations
Experimental fluorescence measurements and thrombin binding assays
experiments_or_evidence: Experimentally observed differences in thrombin binding affinities among AnMeInd, AnMeBtz, and AnBtz-modified TBA
Fluorescence enhancement upon thrombin binding for AnBtz, correlated with reduced solvent accessibility
Suppression of nonradiative decay via excited-state protonation (photobasicity) in AnBtz when bound to thrombin
assumptions: The thrombin binding pocket’s aromatic/charged residue composition governs probe accommodation.
TICT behavior inferred from TD-DFT is transferable to solution and bound-state environments.
Solvent accessibility changes upon binding are sufficient to modulate excited-state protonation kinetics.
limitations: No explicit quantification of kinetic rates for excited-state proton transfer or TICT.
Limited experimental validation of predicted probe binding poses from D-ABMD.
Study focuses exclusively on TBA–thrombin; generalizability to other aptamer–protein pairs remains untested.

gap_signals.text: Limited experimental validation of predicted probe binding poses from D-ABMD.
No explicit quantification of kinetic rates for excited-state proton transfer or TICT.
Study focuses exclusively on TBA–thrombin; generalizability to other aptamer–protein pairs remains untested.
Difficulties predicting the functions of novel probe modifications, especially in different cellular environments.

important_claims.claim: AnBtz functions as a photobase — undergoing excited-state protonation in solvent-exposed environments (unbound), leading to nonradiative decay — but this pathway is suppressed upon thrombin binding due to reduced solvent accessibility, enhancing fluorescence.
important_claims.evidence: Combined computational (TD-DFT, MD) and experimental fluorescence data show enhanced emission only upon binding, correlating with predicted burial and desolvation of AnBtz in the thrombin pocket.

important_claims.claim: Cationic probes AnMeInd and AnMeBtz exhibit inherent TICT tendency, but methyl substituents in AnMeInd reduce aptamer scaffold contacts and alter binding location to increase rigidity and suppress TICT upon binding.
important_claims.evidence: TD-DFT predicts TICT for both cationic probes; D-ABMD and classical MD show distinct binding poses and reduced mobility/solvent exposure for AnMeInd vs. AnMeBtz in the thrombin pocket.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides mechanistic design principles (charge state, substituent effects, microenvironmental control) and a validated multiscale computational workflow for developing environment-sensitive fluorescent nucleic acid probes.
